products.name: watsonx Orchestrate
products.tagline: AI agents that do the work, not chatbots that talk about it.
products.pattern: Companies scaling shared services after a headcount freeze find that cross-system work stalls. Their people spend the day copying data between SAP, ServiceNow, and email because no single bot spans all three.
products.gap: When they try to automate back-office work, they get stuck at the system boundary. One-system bots break the moment a process crosses into another tool.
products.proof: Peers have automated 60% of tier-1 HR and finance volume without rip-and-replace, freeing analysts to do the work they were actually hired for.
products.validation: One real cross-system process automated in 14 days, with time saved measured.
products.topObjection: We already have RPA / a chatbot / one-system agents.
products.objectionResponse: Ours is the one that spans systems and actually acts. RPA breaks at the system boundary. Chatbots talk. Orchestrate executes across SAP, ServiceNow, and email in one flow. The question isn't whether you have automation -- it's whether you have automation that completes the work without a human picking up the handoff.
compellingEvents: Headcount freeze with work piling up
Cost reduction target with a date
products.killerQuestion: What's the back-office work where one ticket touches five systems and three humans?

Situation: Walk me through the last cross-functional request that took longer than it should have. Who touched it, in what order?
Where are your people spending time today on work that doesn't feel like the job you hired them for?
What back-office work in your team spans three or more systems and at least two humans?
Pain: When a process spans SAP, ServiceNow, and email, what breaks? Where does it stall?
Tell me about the last time a routine request escalated to a manager. Why did it have to?
What work would you stop doing tomorrow if you could? Why can't you?
Impact: If you could give back ten hours a week per analyst, what would they do with it that actually moves the business?
What does one stalled HR ticket or finance approval cost you, end to end, including the chase?
Critical Event: What's on your CFO's desk this quarter that requires this team to operate more efficiently?
What did you commit to this year that you're not on track to hit at the current operating model?
Decision: If we agreed today that this is worth doing, what's the path to actually getting it bought? Who else needs to nod?

universalFollowUps: Can you give me an example?
What have you tried already, and what happened?
If nothing changes, what does this look like in 12 months?
Who else in the org feels this the most?

products.name: IBM Bob (watsonx Code Assistant)
products.tagline: Engineer-grade AI for the languages your business actually runs on.
products.pattern: Engineering orgs with cloud migration mandates are finding the real blocker isn't infrastructure. It's that 2 or 3 senior engineers understand the legacy codebase, and everyone else is afraid to touch it.
products.gap: When they try to modernize, they hit the knowledge problem. Documentation takes months, retires with the experts, and never reflects what the code actually does today.
products.proof: Teams using Bob have onboarded new engineers into legacy COBOL and Java applications in days, not quarters, with confidence in transformation quality.
products.validation: One real codebase, one real translation, side-by-side with their developer, in two weeks.
products.objectionResponse: Copilot is excellent for greenfield development in mainstream languages. It doesn't support COBOL, RPG, or PL/I -- the languages your legacy estate actually runs on. And it has no concept of enterprise governance, security policy, or regulatory controls. Bob works where Copilot stops.
products.killerQuestion: Which part of your code estate would you rebuild from scratch if you had infinite engineers -- but you don't?

Situation: What's in your code estate that the market thinks is 'legacy' but is actually your business?
How are your engineers using AI assistance today? What's working, what isn't?
Which platforms or languages does Copilot or Cursor not support that you wish they did?
Pain: When senior engineers retire, what knowledge walks out the door with them?
What modernization work has been on your roadmap for three years and keeps getting deprioritized? Why?
What's the part of your platform you'd rewrite tomorrow if you had infinite engineers, but you don't?
Impact: What does one unplanned outage on your legacy core actually cost? Customer impact, revenue impact, headline risk?
How much of your engineering budget goes into keeping the lights on versus building new?
Critical Event: What's the regulatory or licensing event that's going to force a decision on the mainframe or legacy estate in the next 18 months?
What modernization commitment did you make to the board that's coming due?
Decision: Who in the org owns the answer to 'are we modernizing or are we milking it'? How is that decision made?

universalFollowUps: Can you give me an example?
What have you tried already, and what happened?
If nothing changes, what does this look like in 12 months?
Who else in the org feels this the most?

products.name: watsonx.governance
products.tagline: The control plane that lets you actually scale AI in a regulated business.
products.pattern: CIOs who have publicly committed to AI at scale are finding that their risk and compliance teams manually review every model before production. That worked at 3 models. It is breaking at 30.
products.gap: When they try to scale AI deployment, every new model requires a custom approval cycle. There's no consistent framework, so approvals take weeks and the compliance team becomes the bottleneck.
products.proof: Regulated-industry peers have cut model approval cycles from 6 weeks to 5 days with automated policy enforcement and evidence capture.
products.validation: One real model put under governance, one policy enforced, one audit trail produced, in 14 days.
products.objectionResponse: You can -- but you'll be building it again in 18 months when the regulator changes the rules. Governance frameworks aren't a one-time build. They're a living system that needs to track evolving regulations, new model types, and shifting organizational policies. We update ours continuously. Your internal team will be chasing that forever.
compellingEvents: AI Act or sectoral regulation deadline
products.killerQuestion: If your CEO had to certify to the board that every AI decision in production is auditable, traceable, and policy-compliant -- could you sign that letter today?

Situation: How many AI or ML models do you have in production today? How many do you think you'll have in 18 months?
Who in your org can answer 'is this model still safe to run'? How long does it take them?
What happens today when a model drifts or a regulator asks for documentation?
Pain: When your CRO or CCO asks 'do we know what every AI system in production is doing,' what's the answer? In how many days can you produce it?
What's your current process for getting a new model approved into production? How long does it actually take, end to end?
Where in the AI lifecycle do you currently have manual spreadsheet handoffs?
Impact: If your CEO had to certify to the board that every AI decision in production is auditable, traceable, and policy-compliant, could you sign that letter today?
What does a missed AI compliance event look like for you? Fines, headline, license risk, customer trust?
Critical Event: Which regulation or sectoral rule is going to land in your market in the next 12 to 18 months that changes how you have to govern AI?
What's the board-level AI commitment that's coming due, and what does it require to demonstrate?
Decision: Who owns AI governance in your org today? CIO, CDO, CRO, Chief Compliance Officer, or a committee with no real chair? That answer alone usually predicts the path.

universalFollowUps: Can you give me an example?
What have you tried already, and what happened?
If nothing changes, what does this look like in 12 months?
Who else in the org feels this the most?

plays.name: Play 1: Inbound Discovery
plays.when: They came to you. Someone filled out a form, clicked an ad, or was referred in.
plays.goal: Qualify the pain, quantify the impact, sketch the decision path, propose a validation.
preCAllPrep: Research: company size, industry, recent news, job postings related to your product area
Write your two-sentence hypothesis before you dial
Confirm the right people are on the call -- pain owner, not just the coordinator
Have the SE Brief template open so you can fill it live

steps.name: The Opening: Up-Front Contract
steps.script:
Appreciate the time. Here's what I'd like to accomplish today: understand your priorities, ask a few pointed questions, share a quick point of view, and recommend a specific next step. If we both see a fit, we'll align on a validation plan. If not, we shake hands. Still good on time?

Then: Okay if I recap what I'm hearing as we go?

And: My goal today is to figure out if there's enough impact here to justify a validation -- and to be honest with you if there isn't.
steps.why: Three signals fire in the buyer's head: this seller has a plan, this seller has a point of view, this seller is willing to walk away. All three lower defenses.

steps.name: Hypothesis Validation
steps.script:
Before we get into your questions, I want to share why I think this conversation makes sense -- and you can tell me if I'm wrong.

Companies like yours in [industry] are usually finding that [pattern]. When they try to [goal], they hit [specific gap], which costs them [impact]. Is that anywhere close to what's going on here, or is the real issue something different?
steps.why: Leads with a point of view instead of 'tell me about yourself.' Invites disagreement, which reveals the real pain faster than open questions.

steps.name: SPICED Discovery
steps.script:
S - SITUATION: Walk me through how [relevant process] works today. Who's involved, in what order?

P - PAIN: When that process hits [known friction point], what actually breaks? How often?

I - IMPACT: If this keeps going for 12 more months unchanged, what does that cost you -- in dollars, hours, or risk events?

Do the math live: So if that's [X hours] per person times [Y people] times [Z weeks], you're looking at roughly [calculated number]. Does that feel right?

CE - CRITICAL EVENT: What's making this a priority right now versus six months ago? Is there a specific date or event this needs to be solved before?

D - DECISION: Walk me through how something like this gets bought. Who else would need to be involved? Who signs?
steps.why: SPICED in order surfaces the full picture. The math moment is the most important 60 seconds -- buyers who do the calculation with you are 3x more likely to advance.

leftSwingDropIns.Impact: I want to make sure I'm not overstating this -- is the cost of not solving this actually significant enough to justify a project, or is it more of an annoyance?
leftSwingDropIns.Critical Event: A lot of teams I talk to say this is important, but then it doesn't get prioritized. What makes this different this time?
leftSwingDropIns.Decision: Help me understand -- if we agreed today that this was worth doing, is there actually a path to getting it approved, or are there budget or political blockers I should know about?

steps.name: The Close: Problem Lock and Path Forward
steps.script:
Lock the problem: So let me make sure I've got this right. You're dealing with [pain], it's costing you [impact], and [CE] is creating pressure to solve it before [date]. Is that an accurate picture?

Recommend a path: Based on what you've told me, I think the right next step is a 14-day validation where we [specific scope]. The goal is [specific success metric]. We'd need [specific inputs from them]. At the end of two weeks, you'd have enough evidence to decide yes or no.

Ask for the decision: Does that feel like the right scope? And can we get [EB name] on a 30-minute call this week to align on whether this is worth doing?
steps.why: Lock before recommending. If the buyer doesn't confirm the pain, any recommendation lands in a vacuum.

steps.name: Same-Day Recap Email
steps.script:
Subject: Recap and proposed next step -- [Company]

[Name], thanks for the time today. Here's what I heard:
- [Pain 1 in their exact words]
- [Pain 2 in their exact words]
- [Quantified impact they gave me]
- Compelling event: [CE with date]

Proposed next step: [Validation scope, 1 sentence]. I'd like to bring [SE name] into a 30-minute call with [EB name] to align on whether this is worth starting.

Does [specific date/time] work?
steps.why: Recap email sent same day is the single strongest signal of seller credibility. It also creates a written record of the pain the buyer confirmed.

steps.name: Exit Criteria: What Must Be True to Advance
steps.script:
Before moving to Design/Propose, confirm:
- Quantified pain confirmed in writing
- Compelling event with a real date
- Economic buyer identified by name
- Decision process sketched
- Next step on the calendar with the right people
steps.why: If any of these are blank, that's your next 72 hours of work -- not the SE follow-up, not the proposal.

plays.name: Play 2: Outbound Discovery
plays.when: You created this meeting. The buyer didn't ask for it.
plays.goal: Earn the right to ask questions by leading with a point of view. Land enough pain to justify a follow-up.
preCAllPrep: Write your four-part hypothesis: Pattern, Gap, Proof, Directed Question
Build your 90-second provocation from the hypothesis
Research 1 specific trigger: earnings call, job posting, news item, or 10-K language
Know which left-swing drop-ins to use if energy drops

steps.name: The Opening: Outbound Up-Front Contract
steps.script:
Thanks for making the time. I know you didn't ask for this call, so I'll be direct: I have a specific hypothesis about [company] that I want to test with you. If I'm wrong, I'll tell you and we'll wrap up early. If I'm right, I'd like 20 minutes to go deeper.

Fair?
steps.why: Acknowledges the cold context. Reduces defensiveness. Sets the expectation that you have something specific, not a product pitch.

steps.name: The Provocation
steps.script:
Here's what I'm seeing with companies like yours:

[PATTERN]: [Observation about what's happening in their industry/company size/situation.]

[TENSION]: [The specific friction that creates -- what breaks down, what gets stuck, what costs money.]

[IMPLICATION]: [What happens if they stay on the current path -- in dollars, risk, or lost ground.]

I noticed [specific trigger from their company] and it made me think you might be hitting this earlier than most. Is that anywhere close to what's actually going on?
steps.why: The provocation earns the right to ask questions. It shows you've done work, you have a point of view, and you're not fishing for information you should already have.

steps.name: Inverted SPICED Discovery
steps.script:
I - IMPACT FIRST: What's the cost of the current state -- in time, dollars, or risk? (Don't assume. Verify.)

P - PAIN: What specifically breaks down? Where does it stall? How often?

S - SITUATION: Help me understand the current setup -- who's involved, what tools, what volume?

CE - CRITICAL EVENT (construct it, don't just discover it): You mentioned [date/event]. Is there a connection between that and solving this? What does it mean if this isn't fixed before then?

D - DECISION: If we got to the point where you wanted to move on this, how does that work in your org?
steps.why: On outbound, lead with Impact. The buyer didn't call you, so don't ask permission-seeking Situation questions first. Land on the cost of staying the same before anything else.

leftSwingDropIns.Impact: I want to gut-check myself -- is this actually painful enough to be worth doing something about, or is it just a fact of life at this point?
leftSwingDropIns.Critical Event: A lot of the companies I talk to have been sitting on this for a while. What's different right now that makes this the time to look at it?
leftSwingDropIns.Decision: Is there a realistic path to getting something like this approved in the next 90 days, or are we talking longer horizon?

steps.name: The Two-Step Ladder Close
steps.script:
Step 1 -- The POV Follow-Up Ask: I'd like to send you a one-pager that maps what I've seen at [comparable company] to what you just described. It's one page, not a deck. Would that be useful?

Step 2 -- The Validation Ask (only if CE is real): If that lands, I'd want to bring my SE in for a 45-minute working session to show you what [specific use case] would look like in your environment. Does that feel like a reasonable next step if the one-pager is relevant?

Book it live: Does [specific date] work for the follow-up call?
steps.why: On outbound, don't jump straight to validation. Earn the right to the next conversation first. The two-step ladder keeps the ask proportional to the trust established.

steps.name: Outbound Recap Email
steps.script:
Subject: The piece I owe you -- [Company]

[Name], good conversation. Two things I'm following up on:

1. [The one-page artifact, named specifically]
2. [Any intro or connection you offered]

What I heard: [Pain in their words]. [Quantified impact if they gave it]. [CE if surfaced].

Next step: [Call/meeting] on [date] to bring in [person]. Calendar invite sent.

Anything else you want me to come prepared with?
steps.why: Short, specific, action-forward. Never send a generic 'great to connect' email after an outbound call.

plays.name: Play 3: Validation
plays.when: The buyer has agreed to a proof of concept or technical validation.
plays.goal: Produce a measurable result in 14 days. Convert that result into a commercial decision.

steps.name: Kickoff (Day 1)
steps.script:
Agenda:
1. Confirm exit criteria in writing (what does 'success' look like? One number or outcome.)
2. Confirm inputs we need from them (data, system access, SME time)
3. Confirm the Day 14 readout is on the calendar with the EB in the room
4. Confirm security review is started in parallel

Exit criteria conversation: I want to make sure we're aligned before we start. If at the end of 14 days we've achieved [specific metric], would that be enough evidence for you to recommend moving to a paid deployment? What would you need to see that we haven't said yet?
steps.why: Exit criteria signed off before work starts is non-negotiable. Without it, the readout becomes a demo, not a decision.

steps.name: Day 14 Readout
steps.script:
Results first: We set out to [exit criteria]. Here's what we achieved: [result against metric].

Business translation: That translates to [dollars / hours / risk events] at your scale.

Recommendation: Based on these results, I'd recommend [Phase 1 scope, timeframe, investment range].

The ask: [EB name], are you prepared to move this to a commercial conversation? If yes, what does that path look like from your side? If not, what would we need to show you?
steps.why: EB in the room on Day 14 is the whole game. If the EB isn't there, push the readout until they can be. A readout to the champion alone adds 6 weeks to the cycle.

plays.name: Play 4: Executive Alignment
plays.when: You have an executive buyer meeting -- either the first time you're meeting them or a critical alignment moment.
plays.goal: Get the EB to confirm the business case, sponsor the path forward, and connect you to procurement.

steps.name: Pre-Meeting: The EB One-Pager
steps.script:
One page. Their logo top-left. IBM small. Their language. Their numbers.

- Headline: the business outcome in their words (not product names)
- Current State: 3 bullets with their numbers
- What Changes: 3 outcome bullets (not features)
- The Path: Validate (14 days) / Deploy (60 days) / Scale (90+ days) with dates
- Investment Range: order-of-magnitude only
- Why Now: their compelling event in their words
- What We Need From You: the one decision you're asking for today
steps.why: EBs don't read decks. They read one-pagers. If you can't fit the business case on one page, you don't understand the deal yet.

steps.name: The 30-Minute Agenda
steps.script:
Minutes 1-3: Set the agenda. 'I want to cover three things: what we learned in the validation, what a path forward looks like, and whether this is the right priority right now. Does that work?'

Minutes 3-10: Champion presents the problem. (Not you. Let the champion own the internal framing.)

Minutes 10-20: IBM presents the results and the path. Use the one-pager.

Minutes 20-28: EB questions and objections.

Minute 28: The ask. 'Based on what you've seen, is this something you want to move forward on? If yes, what's the right next step from your side?'
steps.why: Champion presenting the problem is the most important structural move. It signals internal ownership and keeps IBM from being the vendor.

plays.name: Play 5: Stalled Deal Recovery
plays.when: The deal has gone quiet. 2+ weeks of silence after what seemed like forward momentum.
plays.goal: Diagnose whether the deal is dead, delayed, or just deprioritized -- and take the right action for each.

steps.name: Play 5A: Re-Anchor to the Pain
steps.time: Use first, within 7 days of last contact
steps.script:
Subject: Worth re-anchoring?

[Name], last we talked, [date], you said [specific quote about the pain or CE].

Has anything changed on that since? If the timeline shifted, no problem -- I just don't want to keep nudging you on something that isn't a priority anymore.

If it's still live, I'd like to bring [SE / exec / customer reference] to the next conversation. Worth getting on the calendar?

steps.name: Play 5B: The Diagnostic Breakup
steps.time: Primary breakup -- use after 5A gets no response (7 days)
steps.script:
Subject: Closing the loop on the [initiative] at [Company]

Hi [Name],

Hope you're doing well. It's been a few weeks since our last correspondence, so I wanted to check in on the status of the [X initiative] within [Company]. In my experience, a lapse in communication spanning this duration usually means one of the following:

1. Priorities have shifted and this is no longer planned for [year].
2. Pricing didn't meet expectations. If so, we're happy to work with you on that.
3. You've decided to move forward with another vendor.

Are any of these accurate? If so, it would be helpful receiving your confirmation to ensure we no longer occupy an unfair amount of your time. Looking forward to hearing from you!
steps.why: Forces a yes/no on one of three real reasons. Each answer tells you which play to run: (1) nurture and re-engage at the next trigger, (2) rescope phase 1 or re-anchor value, (3) ask what you missed and who won.

steps.name: Play 5C: Multi-Thread / Executive Escalation
steps.time: When champion has gone fully silent
steps.script:
Check LinkedIn first: still at the company? Still in the same role?

Then email a second contact: 'Wanted to check in on the [project name] work. [Champion] has been quiet -- has the priority shifted, or is there someone else closer to it now?'

If still nothing after 7 days, executive escalation:

Subject: Aligning on next steps for [outcome]

[EB name], I wanted to write directly. We started a conversation with [champion] about [outcome] in [month]. We had alignment on [pain] and a path that looked like [validate > deploy > scale].

We've gone quiet for [time]. Before we close the file, I wanted to make sure that's the right call from your side -- or whether there's a way we can be more useful.

cc-ing [your manager] in case it's useful to bring an exec into the conversation.

objections.response: I could send a deck, but most of it won't apply to you. Twenty minutes of questions and I can send you the one page that actually matches what you're trying to do. Worth it?

Totally fair. Most of the companies I work with weren't looking when we first talked. They were looking nine months later when [trigger]. Want me to check back when [specific trigger] is closer, or is there a better time for a quick conversation?

objections.objection: You're not talking to the right person
objections.response: Good, then you can save me a wrong turn. Who in the [function] organization is thinking about [outcome] right now? I'd still love to keep you in the loop because [reason their function cares].

objections.objection: We're going to build it ourselves
objections.response: Makes sense, a lot of [industry] teams start there. The pattern I see is the first 30% is easy and the last 70% eats the roadmap. Out of curiosity, what's your team's plan for [specific hard part -- governance / cross-system orchestration / hallucination control]?

objections.objection: Budget is tight / It's too expensive
objections.response: Fair. Help me understand: is the concern the total number, the timing of when it hits, or the ROI you'd need to see to justify it? (Answer based on the real version: total = re-scope phase 1; timing = ramped pricing; ROI = re-anchor on the math from discovery.)

Which is exactly why you should be looking at IBM rather than the alternatives. Can you tell me which specific concern is loudest -- data residency, model access, or something else? I'll get our architect on a call with your security team this week.

Good, you should be evaluating options. What's the criteria you're using to decide? I want to make sure we're being evaluated on the things that actually matter to [outcome], not just on a feature checklist.
Help me understand what you're trying to learn in that time. If it's [X], we can do that in two weeks with a validation. If it's [Y], we can do that in a meeting next Tuesday. What's actually open?

Copilot is excellent for greenfield development in mainstream languages. It doesn't support COBOL, RPG, or PL/I -- the languages your legacy estate actually runs on. And it has no enterprise governance, security policy, or regulatory controls. Bob works where Copilot stops.

RPA is excellent for single-system, rules-based tasks. The work I'm describing spans multiple systems -- SAP, ServiceNow, and email -- in a single flow. When a process crosses a system boundary, RPA hands off to a human. Orchestrate completes the work. The question is whether the work you're automating stays in one system or crosses three.

emails.name: Cold Outbound
emails.subject: [Specific trigger] at [Company]
emails.body:
Saw [specific public trigger: 10-K language, new hire, earnings call quote, job rec].

Companies like yours usually hit [specific gap] when they try to [outcome]. The [function] team ends up [pain].

We helped [comparable] move from [before] to [after] in [time]. Different shape, same pattern.

Worth twenty minutes next week to test whether the same pattern shows up for you? I'll come with two questions, not a pitch.

emails.name: Inbound Discovery Recap
emails.subject: Recap and three options for [Company]
emails.body:
Thanks for the time today. Here's what I heard:
- [Pain 1 in their words]
- [Pain 2 in their words]
- [Quantified impact they gave me]

Three ways to move this forward:
1. 30-minute working session with your [function] lead
2. Two-week validation on [use case]
3. Executive intro with [EB] to align on scope

Which fits?

emails.name: Outbound First-Call Recap
emails.subject: The piece I owe you
emails.body:
Good conversation. Two things I'm following up on:
1. [The one-page artifact, named specifically]
2. [The intro you offered to make, named specifically]

Next step we discussed: [meeting] on [date] to bring in [person]. Calendar invite sent.

Anything else you want me to bring?

emails.name: Stalled: The Nudge (Play 5A)
emails.subject: Worth re-anchoring?
emails.body:
Last we talked, [date], you said [specific quote about the pain or compelling event].

Has anything changed on that since? If the timeline shifted, no problem -- I just don't want to keep nudging you on something that isn't a priority anymore.

If it's still live, I'd like to bring [SE / executive / customer reference] to the next conversation. Worth getting on the calendar?

emails.name: Stalled: Diagnostic Breakup (Play 5B) -- USE THIS FIRST
emails.subject: Closing the loop on the [initiative] at [Company]
emails.body:
Hi [Name],

Hope you're doing well. It's been a few weeks since our last correspondence, so I wanted to check in on the status of the [X initiative] within [Company]. In my experience, a lapse in communication spanning this duration usually means one of the following:

1. Priorities have shifted and this is no longer planned for [year].
2. Pricing didn't meet expectations. If so, we're happy to work with you on that.
3. You've decided to move forward with another vendor.

Are any of these accurate? If so, it would be helpful receiving your confirmation to ensure we no longer occupy an unfair amount of your time. Looking forward to hearing from you!

emails.name: Stalled: Permission Breakup (alternate)
emails.subject: Closing the loop
emails.body:
I haven't heard back, which usually means one of three things: (1) it's not a priority anymore, (2) someone else is driving it, or (3) the timing is just off.

Whichever it is, no offense taken -- just want to know so I can stop bugging you. A one-line reply works.

If it's (2), I'd still love an intro to whoever is closer to it now.

emails.name: Stalled: Executive Escalation (Play 5C)
emails.subject: Aligning on next steps for [outcome]
emails.body:
[EB name], I wanted to write directly. We started a conversation with [champion] about [outcome] in [month]. We had alignment on [pain] and a path that looked like [validation > deploy > scale].

We've gone quiet for [time]. Before we close the file, I wanted to make sure that's the right call from your side -- or whether there's a way we can be more useful.

Happy to come back with a tighter scope if that helps. cc-ing [manager name] in case it's useful to bring an exec into the conversation.

hypothesisTemplates.twoSentence: Companies like [theirs] in [industry] are usually [observation about what is happening]. When they try to [scale / govern / automate / etc.], they hit [specific problem], which costs them [time / dollars / risk].

fourPart.pattern: Pattern: [Observation about what companies like theirs are experiencing]
fourPart.gap: Gap: [The specific thing that breaks down when they try to solve it]
fourPart.proof: Proof: [One-sentence reference to what peers have achieved]
fourPart.question: Question: [The one question that proves or disproves the hypothesis in the first 5 minutes]
